Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'm 25, working to support myself with limited resources, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could—late nights, early mornings, bus rides. I learn best by listening, so I'd often record lectures or find audio versions of materials and play them while doing other things. There wasn't really a 'routine' in the traditional sense; it was more like survival mode studying.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming for hours, but then I'd go days without touching anything because of work or just being exhausted. I tried to be consistent, but with 14 online courses and everything else going on, regular times just weren't realistic for me. It was always reactive rather than proactive.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when anxiety about falling behind got too intense. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't accomplish much. Notifications about deadlines were my main trigger—without them, I'd probably forget the LMS existed for days.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of logging in every morning with my coffee before work. It lasted maybe two weeks before life got in the way again. The habit formed because I started associating it with my morning routine, but one crisis at work broke the chain and I never really recovered that consistency. I wish it had stuck.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline—I could listen to them while doing other things, which fit my auditory learning style perfectly. I'd watch about an hour's worth regularly because I could absorb information that way without sitting still. Discussion posts from other students sometimes helped clarify things too, even though I never posted myself. Anything I could consume passively while multitasking was gold for me.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums for posting—I just couldn't find the energy or time to formulate responses when I was barely keeping up with assignments. Reading materials that were dense PDFs got skipped a lot because I needed to hear information, not read walls of text. Some supplementary resources felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on when I was already drowning in the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it matched how I actually learn. If I could listen to it or if it was clearly going to be on an exam, I'd prioritize it. Anything that felt like busywork or required me to sit and read for hours without audio alternatives just didn't make the cut when I was stretched so thin.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me. When you're juggling 14 courses with limited resources and time, optional means 'skip this.' Required activities got my attention because they had consequences, but even then I only completed about 59% of assignments. The psychological weight of 'required' versus 'optional' was massive—one triggered my anxiety enough to act, the other didn't.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen on repeat during my commute or while working. I had a cheap notebook where I'd jot down key points I heard, and I'd review those notes constantly. The LMS was just where I grabbed materials from; the real learning happened in my headphones and in that worn-out notebook.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was actively studying—listening to downloaded lectures, reviewing my handwritten notes, even quizzing myself verbally—but I never logged into the LMS once. My exam prep was almost entirely offline because I'd already gathered what I needed. The system would show me as inactive when I was actually putting in hours of work in my own way.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, convert it to audio, and then listen to it maybe ten or fifteen times over the following weeks. The LMS would show one view, but that material was living in my head through constant repetition. Same with any PDFs I could get text-to-speech to read to me—one download, endless offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, I'd freeze up completely—I'd know I needed to engage but couldn't make myself do it. Financial stress would consume my mental energy, leaving nothing for coursework. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment, it was like being paralyzed while watching deadlines fly past.
Q13: Constantly. With 14 courses and limited resources, I was always triaging. Fatigue from work meant I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about course material would make me avoid it entirely rather than ask for help. When rent was due or work got demanding, the LMS became the thing I sacrificed first because it felt like the only flexible part of my life.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, running through work problems, just zoning out from exhaustion. My eyes were on the screen but nothing was going in. Those sessions probably looked like engagement in the data, but they were hollow.
Q15: I postponed exams and assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made it worse—the more important something was, the more terrifying it felt to attempt it and potentially fail. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was more prepared, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a vicious cycle that my CBT sessions helped me understand, but understanding it didn't always stop it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then see a deadline approaching and suddenly log in frantically. My engagement was completely deadline-driven—without that external pressure, I'd drift. The problem was I'd often realize too late that I couldn't complete things properly in the time remaining, which explains my attendance and completion rates.
Q17: When I got feedback showing I'd done well on an assignment—like that 97% score—it motivated me briefly. It reminded me I was capable when I actually engaged. But negative feedback or silence made me retreat. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was scared of what I'd see. The feedback loop was broken because my anxiety made me avoid the very information that could help me.
Q18: The course structure honestly overwhelmed me. With 14 courses running simultaneously, keeping track of different structures, deadlines, and expectations was a nightmare. Some courses had clear weekly patterns which helped, but others were all over the place. I needed more predictability and audio-friendly materials than most courses provided. The structure assumed students had time and resources I just didn't have.
Q19: Courses that front-loaded video content made engagement easier because I could download and listen on my schedule. What made it harder was when participation required synchronous attendance—my 10% attendance rate shows how impossible that was with my work schedule. Dense reading requirements without audio alternatives were barriers too. The design assumed a traditional student lifestyle I couldn't live.
Q20: That describes me somewhat. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, browse materials—the LMS would show activity—but then I'd fail to submit work because I ran out of time or got paralyzed by anxiety about doing it wrong. High activity can mean someone is trying desperately but hitting walls that aren't visible in the data. Engagement and output aren't the same thing when life is complicated.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone downloads everything early and studies offline, they'd look inactive but could perform well. That wasn't quite my situation since my exam scores suffered, but my high assignment scores when I did submit show I understood the material. The LMS activity doesn't capture learning that happens away from the screen, which for auditory learners like me is most of it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long pages stay open. But they miss everything meaningful: whether you actually understood what you watched, whether you were present mentally, all the offline studying, the personal circumstances affecting your engagement. They capture the skeleton of engagement without any of the flesh. They're measuring shadows, not the actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means the material is actually getting into my head and connecting to something meaningful. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, not just exposing myself to them. For me, real engagement often happens when I'm walking and listening to a lecture, making connections in my mind, talking through ideas to myself. It's internal and active, not just clicking through screens.
Q24: So much. The LMS wouldn't show my hours of audio review, my notebook full of handwritten summaries, the times I explained concepts to myself out loud to test understanding. It wouldn't show my anxiety attacks before exams or how my CBT sessions helped me keep going when I wanted to quit. It wouldn't capture that I'm a 25-year-old with limited resources fighting to stay in school while working. The data shows a failing student; it doesn't show someone fighting hard in ways the system can't see.
